title: Arjo Flowtron Excel Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Sleeve Not Inflating or Low Pressure
description: Sleeve not inflating, slow inflation, or low pressure caused by connection issues, garment problems, or external pneumatic faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on an alternate SCD or therapy paused safely.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and observe a full cycle. Expected: Confirm sleeve does not inflate, inflates slowly, or fails to reach pressure. Why it matters: Confirms true device issue vs normal cycle timing.

steps.title: Check Sleeve (Garment) Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure tubing is fully seated in the pump port. Disconnect and reconnect firmly. Expected: Secure, tight connection with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose connections are the most common cause of no inflation. If corrected and inflation returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Sleeve and Tubing for Leaks or Damage
steps.instructions: Check for holes, tears, kinks, or disconnected tubing. Run hand along tubing while unit attempts inflation. Expected: No air leaks, hissing, or visible damage. Why it matters: Air leaks prevent pressure buildup. If damaged → replace sleeve/tubing and retest.

steps.title: Verify Correct Sleeve Type and Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct garment type is used and properly wrapped on the limb. Expected: Properly fitted sleeve with secure closure. Why it matters: Improper fit or incompatible garment may prevent proper inflation or pressure sensing.

steps.title: Check for Kinked or Obstructed Tubing
steps.instructions: Ensure tubing is not pinched under bed rails, mattress, or equipment. Expected: Tubing routed freely without obstruction. Why it matters: Blocked airflow reduces or prevents inflation. If corrected → retest and stop if resolved.

steps.title: Observe Pump Operation (Sound and Air Output)
steps.instructions: Listen for pump cycling and feel for airflow at the port (briefly disconnect tubing if needed). Expected: Audible pump operation and detectable airflow. Why it matters: Confirms whether the pump is generating pressure. If no sound or airflow → likely internal pump or control issue.

steps.title: Check for Alarm Indicators or Fault Messages
steps.instructions: Observe display or indicators for pressure or connection alarms. Expected: No active alarms during operation. Why it matters: System may inhibit inflation if it detects a fault condition.

steps.title: Perform Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 10–15 seconds, then power back on. Expected: System resets and resumes normal cycling. Why it matters: Clears minor control or detection faults. If resolved → stop troubleshooting.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported SCD sleeve not inflating on Flowtron Excel unit.
CCR.Cause: Leak identified in sleeve tubing causing inability to maintain pressure.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced sleeve and tubing set; verified normal inflation cycle and pressure.

title: Arjo Flowtron Excel Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Pressure alarm conditions
description: Troubleshooting pressure alarms caused by tubing, garment, or connection issues affecting inflation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on alternate DVT prophylaxis.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm Condition
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and observe the alarm behavior during a cycle. Expected: Alarm triggers during inflation or pressure hold phase. Why it matters: Confirms true pressure-related fault vs user misunderstanding.

steps.title: Check Sleeve (Garment) Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure tubing is fully inserted into the pump port. Disconnect and reconnect firmly. Expected: Secure connection with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose connections are the most common cause of pressure alarms. If corrected and alarm clears → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Sleeve and Tubing for Leaks or Damage
steps.instructions: Check entire length of tubing and garment for holes, tears, or disconnections. Listen for escaping air during inflation. Expected: No audible leaks or visible damage. Why it matters: Air leaks prevent pressure buildup and trigger alarms. If damaged → replace garment and retest.

steps.title: Check for Kinks or Obstructions in Tubing
steps.instructions: Inspect tubing for sharp bends, compression under bed rails, or pinching. Expected: Tubing is straight and unobstructed. Why it matters: Restricted airflow can mimic pressure failure conditions. If corrected and alarm clears → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Correct Garment Type and Size
steps.instructions: Ensure compatible Flowtron sleeves are being used and properly sized for the patient. Expected: Properly fitted, manufacturer-approved garment. Why it matters: Incorrect or incompatible sleeves affect pressure regulation. If corrected → retest and confirm normal operation.

steps.title: Test Alternate Sleeve (Known Good)
steps.instructions: Replace with a known-good sleeve and tubing set. Expected: Normal inflation cycle without alarms. Why it matters: Quickly isolates accessory vs device issue. If alarm resolves → original sleeve is faulty.

steps.title: Inspect Pump Ports for Debris or Damage
steps.instructions: Check air outlet ports for blockage, dust, or damage. Expected: Clean, unobstructed ports. Why it matters: Blocked ports can prevent accurate pressure delivery.

steps.title: Perform Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 10 seconds, then restart. Expected: System resets and alarm may clear. Why it matters: Clears transient faults or sensor misreads.

CCR.Complaint: Unit alarming for pressure during use; sleeves not inflating properly.
CCR.Cause: Leaking sleeve tubing preventing adequate pressure buildup.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced sleeve with known-good set; verified normal inflation and no alarms.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Arjo Flowtron Excel Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Pump Not Powering On
description: Pump not turning on due to power supply, cord, outlet, or external electrical issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on alternate DVT prophylaxis.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Press the power button and observe for any lights, sounds, or display activity. Expected: No response from the unit. Why it matters: Confirms true no-power condition vs display-only failure.

steps.title: Check Power Cord Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the power cord is fully inserted into the device and wall outlet. Disconnect and firmly reconnect both ends. Expected: Secure connection with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose cords are a common cause of power loss. If unit powers on → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Test the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Plug a known working device into the same outlet. Expected: Outlet provides power. If no power → move SCD to a known-good outlet and retest. Why it matters: Eliminates facility power issues.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord for Damage
steps.instructions: Check for cuts, fraying, bent prongs, or exposed wiring. Expected: Cord is intact and undamaged. If damaged → remove from service and replace cord. If replaced and unit powers on → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Power Switch / Button Function
steps.instructions: Press the power button multiple times and observe for tactile response or sticking. Expected: Button feels normal and responsive. Why it matters: Mechanical button failure can prevent power-up.

steps.title: Inspect for External Indicators
steps.instructions: Look for any faint lights, flickering, or intermittent power behavior when plugging in. Expected: No signs of partial power. Why it matters: Intermittent signs may indicate internal power supply issues.

steps.title: Check for Blown Fuse (If Accessible Externally)
steps.instructions: If the unit has an external fuse holder, inspect and replace with the correct rating if blown. Expected: Fuse intact. Why it matters: A blown fuse is a common external failure point. If replaced and unit powers on → stop troubleshooting.

CCR.Complaint: SCD pump would not power on when plugged in.
CCR.Cause: Failed power cord identified during inspection.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced power cord and confirmed normal operation.

helpfulDetails: Any indicator lights present or absent
Final device status (operational or sent for repair)

title: Arjo Flowtron Excel Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Uneven Inflation Between Chambers
description: Troubleshooting uneven chamber inflation caused by tubing restrictions, garment defects, or external pneumatic issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on alternate DVT prophylaxis.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run the pump and observe a full inflation cycle across all chambers. Expected: Chambers inflate sequentially and evenly. Why it matters: Confirms true uneven inflation vs normal compression pattern.

steps.title: Check Garment (Sleeve) Application
steps.instructions: Ensure the sleeve is properly wrapped and secured around the limb with no twisting or folding. Expected: Even contact along the entire limb. Why it matters: Improper application can restrict airflow and cause uneven inflation.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing Connections
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect tubing at both the pump and garment ports. Ensure connections are fully seated and secure. Expected: Tight, leak-free connections. Why it matters: Partial connections can reduce pressure to specific chambers.

steps.title: Check Tubing for Kinks or Obstructions
steps.instructions: Run hands along tubing and visually inspect for kinks, compression under bed rails, or pinching. Expected: Tubing is straight and unobstructed. Why it matters: Restricted airflow affects downstream chamber inflation.

steps.title: Inspect Garment for Internal Defects
steps.instructions: Check for visible wear, stiff sections, or areas that fail to expand during inflation. Gently manipulate chambers while running a cycle. Expected: All chambers inflate uniformly. Why it matters: Internal chamber failure or delamination can prevent proper inflation sequence.

steps.title: Swap with a Known-Good Garment
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good sleeve to the pump and observe operation. Expected: Even inflation across all chambers. Why it matters: Isolates whether the issue is garment-related or pump-related.

steps.title: Check Pump Output Ports
steps.instructions: If dual-channel unit, test each port independently with a known-good garment. Expected: Consistent inflation from each port. Why it matters: Identifies if one channel is underperforming.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 10 seconds, and power back on. Expected: Normal operation resumes. Why it matters: Clears minor control or sequencing faults.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported SCD sleeve inflating unevenly between chambers during therapy.
CCR.Cause: Faulty garment with internal chamber restriction identified during testing.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced sleeve and verified normal sequential inflation across all chambers.

helpfulDetails: Tubing checked for kinks or compression
Final device status (working / removed from service)

title: Arjo Flowtron Excel Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Internal compressor failure
description: Pump runs with little/no pressure or no inflation despite proper setup, suggesting possible internal compressor failure after external causes are ruled out.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on alternate DVT prophylaxis.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and observe operation during a full cycle. Listen for compressor activity and check for sleeve inflation. Expected: Compressor runs but no pressure builds, or compressor is silent with no inflation. Why it matters: Confirms true performance issue vs setup or accessory problem.

steps.title: Check Power and Unit Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit powers on fully with normal indicators (lights/display). Expected: Unit powers on consistently with no power interruptions. Why it matters: Low or unstable power can mimic compressor failure.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing Connections
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect tubing at the pump and garment ports. Ensure secure fit. Expected: Tight, fully seated connections. Why it matters: Loose or partially connected tubing prevents pressure buildup. If corrected and inflation returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Sleeve (Garment) and Tubing for Leaks
steps.instructions: Check for holes, tears, or disconnections. Listen for escaping air during operation. Expected: No visible damage or audible leaks. Why it matters: External leaks are the most common cause of no pressure. If leak found and corrected → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Swap with Known-Good Sleeve and Tubing
steps.instructions: Replace the sleeve and tubing with a verified working set. Expected: Normal inflation with known-good accessories. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is accessory-related or internal to the pump. If issue resolves → original accessories are defective.

steps.title: Check for Kinks or Obstructions in Tubing
steps.instructions: Ensure tubing is not kinked, pinched, or compressed along its length. Expected: Free, unobstructed airflow path. Why it matters: Restricted airflow can prevent pressure from reaching the sleeve. If corrected and function returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Listen for Compressor Operation
steps.instructions: With tubing connected, listen for compressor motor sound during inflation phase. Expected: Audible compressor cycling during operation. No sound → possible compressor or motor failure. Sound present but no pressure → possible internal leak or compressor inefficiency. Why it matters: Helps differentiate electrical vs pneumatic internal failure.

CCR.Complaint: SCD sleeves not inflating; no compression therapy observed during use.
CCR.Cause: Internal compressor failure suspected after confirming power, tubing, and sleeves were functional with known-good components.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, labeled out of service, and sent to Biomed shop/vendor for compressor repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: No pressure felt at tubing output
Compressor silent or running without output

title: Arjo Hydroven Series Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Garment Not Recognized
description: Troubleshooting garment detection issues caused by connection problems, incompatible sleeves, or pneumatic faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on alternate DVT prophylaxis.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and connect the garment. Observe for recognition or error indication. Expected: Unit fails to detect garment or does not initiate therapy. Why it matters: Confirms true detection issue vs normal startup delay.

steps.title: Check Garment Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect and firmly reconnect the tubing to the device port. Ensure it is fully seated. Expected: Secure, tight connection with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose or partial connections are the most common cause of non-recognition. If garment is recognized → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing and Connectors
steps.instructions: Check tubing for kinks, cracks, or damaged connectors. Inspect the port for debris or bent pins (if applicable). Expected: Clean, intact tubing and connectors with no obstruction. Why it matters: Airflow restriction or connector damage can prevent proper detection. If corrected and recognized → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Garment Type
steps.instructions: Verify the garment being used is compatible with the Hydroven Series (correct model and type). Expected: Approved garment type connected. Why it matters: Some systems will not recognize incompatible or incorrect garments. If incorrect garment replaced and recognized → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Swap with a Known Good Garment
steps.instructions: Connect a known working garment to the unit. Expected: Device recognizes the known good garment. Why it matters: Helps determine if the issue is with the garment or the device. If new garment works → original garment is faulty. If issue persists → continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect for Air Leaks During Startup
steps.instructions: Run the unit and listen for air leaks at the connection or along the tubing. Expected: No audible leaks. Why it matters: Leaks can prevent pressure sensing required for garment detection.

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, disconnect power if applicable, wait 30 seconds, then restart. Reconnect the garment and retest. Expected: Device resets and attempts normal detection. Why it matters: Clears minor control or detection faults. If resolved → stop troubleshooting.

CCR.Complaint: SCD not recognizing garment and therapy not starting.
CCR.Cause: Faulty garment tubing preventing proper pressure detection.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced garment with known good unit; device function restored.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent to repair)

title: Arjo Hydroven Series Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Pressure alarm conditions
description: Pressure alarms caused by leaks, blocked tubing, improper garment connection, or external airflow restrictions preventing proper inflation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on alternate DVT prophylaxis.

steps.title: Verify the Alarm Condition
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and observe when the pressure alarm occurs during a full cycle. Expected: Alarm occurs during inflation or pressure hold phase. Why it matters: Confirms true pressure issue vs user misunderstanding or normal cycling.

steps.title: Check Garment (Sleeve) Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect and firmly reconnect the tubing to the device. Ensure it is fully seated. Expected: Tight, secure connection with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose connections are the most common cause of pressure alarms. If alarm clears → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing for Kinks or Obstructions
steps.instructions: Trace the entire tubing from pump to garment. Remove any kinks, compression, or pinching. Expected: Tubing is straight and unobstructed. Why it matters: Blocked airflow can trigger high-pressure alarms. If corrected and alarm clears → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Leaks in Tubing or Garment
steps.instructions: Inspect tubing and sleeve for holes, tears, or disconnections. Listen for escaping air during inflation. Expected: No visible damage or audible leaks. Why it matters: Air leaks prevent proper pressure buildup and trigger alarms. If leak found and corrected → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Correct Garment Type and Compatibility
steps.instructions: Ensure the sleeve being used is appropriate for the Hydroven system and properly sized. Expected: Compatible garment connected correctly. Why it matters: Incorrect or incompatible garments can cause abnormal pressure readings. If corrected → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Blocked or Contaminated Ports
steps.instructions: Inspect the device air outlet port for debris, lint, or obstruction. Expected: Port is clean and unobstructed. Why it matters: Blocked ports restrict airflow and cause pressure faults. If cleared and alarm resolves → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Test with Known-Good Garment and Tubing
steps.instructions: Replace current sleeve and tubing with a known-good set and run a cycle. Expected: Normal operation with no alarms. Why it matters: Helps isolate whether the issue is accessory-related or device-related. If issue resolves → faulty garment/tubing identified.

CCR.Complaint: Unit alarming with pressure fault during inflation cycle.
CCR.Cause: Air leak identified in compression sleeve tubing causing inability to maintain pressure.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced defective sleeve and tubing; verified normal operation with no alarms.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (in service or removed)

title: Arjo Hydroven Series Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Cycle Timing Errors
description: Abnormal inflation/deflation timing, inconsistent cycle duration, or therapy not following expected sequence due to setup, garment, or external airflow issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on alternate DVT prophylaxis.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and observe a full compression cycle from start to finish. Expected: Confirm irregular timing, skipped phases, or inconsistent inflation/deflation intervals. Why it matters: Ensures the issue is real and not normal variation in therapy modes.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Therapy Mode/Settings
steps.instructions: Check that the correct mode and settings are selected (if applicable for model configuration). Expected: Proper therapy mode with consistent cycle pattern. Why it matters: Incorrect mode selection can appear as timing irregularities.

steps.title: Check Garment (Sleeve) Application
steps.instructions: Ensure sleeves are properly applied, not twisted, folded, or overly tight. Expected: Even, smooth wrap around the limb. Why it matters: Improper application can affect pressure delivery and alter cycle timing.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing Connections
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect tubing at both the pump and garment ports. Ensure a secure fit. Expected: Tight, fully seated connections with no looseness. Why it matters: Airflow inconsistencies from loose connections can disrupt cycle timing.

steps.title: Check for Kinks or Restrictions in Tubing
steps.instructions: Trace the full length of tubing and remove any kinks, compression, or pinching. Expected: Tubing is straight and unobstructed. Why it matters: Restricted airflow can delay inflation/deflation phases.

steps.title: Inspect Garment and Tubing for Leaks
steps.instructions: Check for holes, cracks, or loose fittings. Listen for air leaks during operation. Expected: No audible leaks and intact components. Why it matters: Air leaks can prolong inflation time and disrupt normal cycle timing.

steps.title: Swap with Known-Good Garment and Tubing
steps.instructions: Replace sleeves and tubing with a verified working set. Expected: Cycle timing returns to normal. Why it matters: Confirms whether issue is accessory-related vs device-related.

steps.title: Observe for Consistency Across Multiple Cycles
steps.instructions: Run several cycles and monitor timing stability. Expected: Consistent, repeatable cycle timing. Why it matters: Intermittent timing issues may indicate developing internal faults.

CCR.Complaint: SCD reported to have irregular compression timing and inconsistent cycle duration.
CCR.Cause: Faulty garment tubing causing intermittent airflow restriction affecting cycle timing.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced tubing and garment; verified normal cycle timing across multiple cycles.

helpfulDetails: Full cycle observed and timing behavior noted
Tubing inspected for kinks or restrictions
No unusual noises or airflow inconsistencies
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Arjo Hydroven Series Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Display or Keypad Failure
description: Troubleshooting unresponsive keypad or display issues due to power, interface, or external causes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on alternate DVT prophylaxis.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and attempt to use the keypad and observe the display. Expected: Confirm no display, dim display, or unresponsive buttons. Why it matters: Confirms true interface failure vs user operation error.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Cord
steps.instructions: Ensure the power cord is fully seated in both the device and wall outlet. Test outlet with a known working device. Expected: Stable power to the unit with no interruption. Why it matters: Inconsistent or no power can cause display or keypad failure.

steps.title: Inspect for Standby or Power Mode Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is not in standby or partially powered state. Cycle power OFF, wait 10 seconds, then power ON. Expected: Full startup sequence with active display. Why it matters: System lockups can present as display or keypad failure.

steps.title: Check Display Visibility Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect screen for brightness, contrast, or viewing angle issues. Look for very faint text or backlight failure. Expected: Clear, readable display under normal lighting. Why it matters: Backlight failure may appear as a blank screen even if the unit is functioning.

steps.title: Inspect Keypad for Physical Issues
steps.instructions: Check for stuck buttons, fluid ingress, damage, or debris under keypad overlay. Press each key individually. Expected: Buttons move freely and respond when pressed. Why it matters: Physical obstruction or contamination can prevent input registration.

steps.title: Check for Alarm or Locked State
steps.instructions: Observe if the unit is in an alarm condition or locked mode preventing input. Expected: No lockout preventing keypad use. Why it matters: Some conditions may disable input until cleared.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections (if applicable)
steps.instructions: If the unit uses detachable control panels or connectors, ensure all external connections are secure and undamaged. Expected: Secure, intact connections with no bent pins or loose cables. Why it matters: Interface communication issues can mimic keypad/display failure.

CCR.Complaint: SCD display blank and keypad not responding during use.
CCR.Cause: Failed display/backlight assembly after external power and keypad checks passed.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent to vendor for repair.

helpfulDetails: Any signs of fluid ingress or physical damage

title: Arjo Hydroven Series Sequential Compression Device (SCD) - Pump Not Inflating Properly
description: Troubleshooting sleeves not inflating, weak inflation, or incomplete compression due to connection issues, leaks, or airflow restrictions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient placed on alternate DVT prophylaxis.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and observe a full cycle with sleeves connected. Expected: Confirm no inflation, weak inflation, or incomplete chamber filling. Why it matters: Confirms true issue vs normal cycle timing or perception.

steps.title: Check Power and Unit Operation
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit powers on fully and you can hear the pump/compressor running. Expected: Audible pump operation during inflation phase. Why it matters: No sound may indicate a deeper internal issue rather than airflow restriction. If pump is silent → escalate to repair.

steps.title: Inspect Garment (Sleeve) Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect and firmly reconnect tubing to the device. Ensure it is fully seated. Expected: Secure, tight connection with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose connections are the most common cause of no or low inflation. If inflation returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing for Kinks or Obstructions
steps.instructions: Trace the entire tubing path from pump to sleeve. Remove any kinks, compression, or pinching. Expected: Tubing is straight and unobstructed. Why it matters: Restricted airflow prevents proper pressure delivery. If corrected and inflation returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Air Leaks in Tubing or Garment
steps.instructions: Inspect for cracks, holes, or disconnected segments. Listen for hissing during inflation. Expected: No audible leaks or visible damage. Why it matters: Air leaks reduce pressure and prevent full inflation. If leak found → replace tubing or garment → retest.

steps.title: Verify Proper Sleeve Application
steps.instructions: Ensure sleeve is properly wrapped, not twisted, folded, or overly tight. Expected: Even contact along the limb with no restriction points. Why it matters: Improper application can restrict chamber expansion and mimic low pressure. If corrected and inflation normalizes → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Garment and Tubing Set
steps.instructions: Swap with a known working sleeve and tubing. Expected: Normal inflation with replacement set. Why it matters: Quickly isolates accessory vs device issue. If resolved → faulty garment/tubing removed from service.

steps.title: Check for Blocked or Contaminated Ports
steps.instructions: Inspect pump ports for debris, dust, or obstruction. Expected: Clean, unobstructed ports. Why it matters: Blocked ports can restrict airflow at the source.

CCR.Complaint: SCD sleeves not inflating properly during use.
CCR.Cause: Leak identified in garment tubing causing pressure loss.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced tubing and verified normal inflation cycle.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested and unit powered on
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Arjo Maxi Move Series Patient Lift - Lift not raising or lowering
description: Lift not moving up or down due to power, battery, handset, or connection issues that prevent actuator operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another lift or surface.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to operate both UP and DOWN functions using the handset. Expected: No movement or inconsistent movement from the lift. Why it matters: Confirms full function failure vs partial control issue.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop Button
steps.instructions: Inspect the emergency stop (E-stop) button. Twist or pull to reset if engaged. Expected: Button is released and unit powers normally. Why it matters: Engaged E-stop completely disables lift movement. If lift operates after reset → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge and Seating
steps.instructions: Verify battery is installed correctly and fully seated in the lift. Check battery charge indicator. Expected: Battery shows adequate charge and is securely connected. Why it matters: Low or disconnected battery prevents actuator movement. Swap with a known good battery if available. If lift operates → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Charger Function (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Place battery on charger and confirm charging indicators activate. Expected: Charger lights indicate normal charging. Why it matters: A failed charger can lead to repeated dead batteries.

steps.title: Check Hand Control (Pendant)
steps.instructions: Inspect handset cable for damage and ensure it is fully connected to the control box. Test all buttons. Expected: Buttons respond and send commands to lift. Why it matters: Damaged or disconnected handset is a common failure point. If available, swap with a known good handset. If lift operates → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Cables and Connections
steps.instructions: Check all visible cables between control box, actuator, and power system. Ensure all connectors are fully seated. Expected: No loose, pinched, or damaged cables. Why it matters: Loose connections interrupt signal or power to actuator.

steps.title: Observe for Motor/Actuator Response
steps.instructions: Activate lift while listening for motor noise. Expected: Motor sound during operation attempt. Motor runs but no movement: Possible mechanical or actuator issue. No motor sound: Possible control box, battery, or wiring issue. Why it matters: Helps isolate electrical vs mechanical failure.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect lift mast, boom, and lifting mechanism for binding, obstruction, or visible damage. Expected: Smooth, unobstructed movement path. Why it matters: Physical binding can prevent actuator movement.

CCR.Complaint: Lift would not raise or lower when attempting to move patient.
CCR.Cause: Discharged battery not supplying sufficient power to actuator.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced battery with charged unit; verified full lift operation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent to repair)

title: Arjo Maxi Move Series Patient Lift - Battery not charging or short runtime
description: Troubleshooting battery charging issues, rapid drain, and power loss on the Arjo Maxi Move patient lift.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another lift or surface.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the lift and observe battery indicator and runtime during operation. Expected: Low battery indication, rapid drain, or failure to charge. Why it matters: Confirms true battery performance issue vs normal usage.

steps.title: Check Charger Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the charger is fully plugged into both the lift and wall outlet and inspect for loose connections. Expected: Solid connection with no movement. Why it matters: Loose connections prevent proper charging. If charging begins, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Wall Outlet Power
steps.instructions: Test the outlet with a known working device. Expected: Outlet provides consistent power. Why it matters: Eliminates facility power issues. If faulty, move to a known-good outlet and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Charger and Cable Condition
steps.instructions: Check charger and cable for damage such as cuts or fraying. Expected: No visible damage. Why it matters: Damaged chargers cannot deliver proper voltage. Replace if needed and retest.

steps.title: Confirm Battery Seating
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall the battery ensuring it is fully seated and latched. Expected: Battery locks securely. Why it matters: Poor contact prevents charging and causes intermittent power. If charging resumes, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Battery Indicator Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe LED indicators during charging. Expected: Active charging indication. Why it matters: No indicator may suggest battery or charger failure.

steps.title: Swap with a Known Good Battery
steps.instructions: Install a known working battery and test operation and charging. Expected: Normal function. Why it matters: Differentiates battery vs charger or lift issue. If resolved, faulty battery identified.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Charger (if available)
steps.instructions: Use a known working charger with the original battery. Expected: Charging begins normally. Why it matters: Confirms charger failure.

steps.title: Evaluate Runtime Under Load
steps.instructions: Operate the lift through several cycles. Expected: Normal runtime. Why it matters: Short runtime indicates battery degradation.

CCR.Complaint: Lift battery not holding charge and requires frequent recharging.
CCR.Cause: Degraded battery confirmed by short runtime and failure to hold charge under load.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced battery with known good unit; verified proper charging and operation.

helpfulDetails: Any visible cable or connector damage
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Arjo Maxi Move Series Patient Lift - Actuator Failure or Uneven Lifting
description: Lift raising unevenly, stopping mid-motion, or not lifting due to actuator, control, or external connection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another lift or surface.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Operate the lift using the handset and observe lifting motion. Expected: One side lifts unevenly, jerky motion, or no movement. Why it matters: Confirms true actuator-related behavior vs user setup issue.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop (E-Stop)
steps.instructions: Ensure the emergency stop button is not engaged. Reset if needed. Expected: Unit powers fully and responds to controls. Why it matters: Engaged E-stop disables actuator movement. If lift operates normally → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Battery Charge and Seating
steps.instructions: Check battery level and ensure it is fully seated in the lift. Swap with a known good battery if available. Expected: Adequate charge and solid battery connection. Why it matters: Low or unstable power can cause weak or uneven actuator movement. If lift operates normally → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Handset and Control Functions
steps.instructions: Test all lift controls (UP/DOWN and any alternate control panel if available). Expected: Consistent response across all control inputs. Why it matters: A faulty handset can mimic actuator failure. If alternate controls work → replace handset.

steps.title: Inspect Lift Arm and Mechanical Linkages
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the boom, spreader bar, and pivot points for binding, obstruction, or misalignment. Expected: Smooth, unobstructed movement with no physical resistance. Why it matters: Mechanical binding can cause uneven lifting or overload one actuator side. If obstruction is found and corrected → retest.

steps.title: Check for Uneven Load or Sling Application
steps.instructions: Ensure the sling is properly attached and weight is evenly distributed. Expected: Balanced load with equal tension on both sides. Why it matters: Improper sling placement can mimic actuator imbalance. If corrected and lift operates normally → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Listen for Actuator Operation
steps.instructions: During lift attempt, listen for actuator motor noise (grinding, clicking, or silence). Expected: Smooth motor sound during lifting. Why it matters: Noise with no movement → possible actuator failure or mechanical binding. No sound → possible actuator or control failure.

steps.title: Inspect Actuator Cables and Connections
steps.instructions: Check visible actuator wiring and connectors for looseness or damage. Ensure all connections are fully seated. Expected: Secure, undamaged connections. Why it matters: Loose or damaged cables can prevent proper actuator function. If connection issue is corrected → retest.

CCR.Complaint: Lift reported raising unevenly and stopping mid-lift during patient transfer.
CCR.Cause: Failed actuator with inconsistent movement despite proper power, load balance, and connections.
CCR.Resolution: Removed lift from service and sent to vendor for actuator replacement.

title: Arjo Maxi Move Series Patient Lift - Hand control not responding
description: Troubleshooting a patient lift hand control that is not responding due to connection, power, or control interface issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another lift or surface.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to operate all functions using the hand control. Expected: No response or intermittent response from buttons. Why it matters: Confirms a true control issue vs misunderstanding of operation.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop (E-Stop)
steps.instructions: Ensure the emergency stop button is not engaged. Reset if needed. Expected: Lift powers fully and responds to controls. Why it matters: E-stop disables all handset input.

steps.title: Verify Battery Charge and Seating
steps.instructions: Check battery level and confirm it is fully seated. Swap with a known good battery if available. Expected: Adequate charge and stable power. Why it matters: Low or unstable power can cause unresponsive controls.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Connection
steps.instructions: Check where the hand control plugs into the lift. Disconnect and firmly reconnect. Inspect for bent pins, debris, or loose fit. Expected: Secure, clean connection with no damage. Why it matters: Loose or damaged connections are the most common cause.

steps.title: Check Cable Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the entire length of the hand control cable for cuts, kinks, or exposed wires. Gently move the cable while pressing buttons to check for intermittent response. Expected: No visible damage and consistent function. Why it matters: Internal wire breaks can cause intermittent or no response.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Hand Control (If Available)
steps.instructions: Swap with a working handset from another unit. Expected: Lift responds normally with replacement control. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is the handset or internal control system.

steps.title: Check for Control Lockout or System Fault Indicators
steps.instructions: Observe for any indicator lights, fault codes, or unusual behavior. Cycle power OFF, wait 10 seconds, then power ON. Expected: Normal startup and control response. Why it matters: System faults can disable input devices.

CCR.Complaint: Lift hand control not responding; unable to raise or lower patient.
CCR.Cause: Faulty hand control cable with intermittent internal break.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced handset and verified full lift operation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent to repair)

title: Arjo Maxi Move Series Patient Lift - Base Legs Not Opening/Closing
description: Base legs not spreading or closing, preventing proper positioning due to power, actuator, control, or obstruction-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another lift or surface.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to open and close the base legs using the hand control. Expected: No movement, slow movement, or uneven leg operation. Why it matters: Confirms true base actuator issue vs operator misuse.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop (E-Stop)
steps.instructions: Ensure the E-stop button is not engaged. Reset if needed. Expected: Full system power restored and controls responsive. Why it matters: E-stop disables all powered movements, including base legs. If legs respond → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Battery Charge and Seating
steps.instructions: Check battery level and ensure it is fully seated. Swap with a known good battery if available. Expected: Adequate charge and stable power. Why it matters: Base leg actuator requires sufficient power; low voltage can prevent movement. If function returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Operation
steps.instructions: Test other functions (lift up/down, tilt, etc.) using the same handset. Expected: Other functions operate normally or also fail. Why it matters: Determines if the issue is isolated to base control or overall handset failure. If all functions fail → suspect handset or control interface. If only base fails → continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Cable and Connection
steps.instructions: Check for loose connection at the control port and inspect cable for damage or pinching. Expected: Secure connection with no visible damage. Why it matters: Damaged or loose wiring can interrupt specific control signals. If corrected → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect the base legs and surrounding area for debris, objects, or mechanical interference. Manually attempt slight movement (without forcing). Expected: Legs move freely with no binding or obstruction. Why it matters: External blockage is a common and easily missed cause. If obstruction removed and function returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Base Mechanism for Binding or Damage
steps.instructions: Look for bent components, misalignment, or signs of stress at pivot points. Expected: Smooth alignment with no visible damage. Why it matters: Mechanical binding can prevent actuator movement even if powered.

steps.title: Listen for Actuator Operation
steps.instructions: Operate the base control and listen for motor/actuator noise. Expected: Audible motor sound during operation. Motor runs but no movement → possible mechanical failure or linkage issue. No motor sound → possible actuator, wiring, or control board issue.

CCR.Complaint: Base legs would not open or close during patient transfer.
CCR.Cause: No actuator response; ruled out battery, handset, and obstruction—suspected base actuator failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed lift from service and sent to biomed shop for actuator evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (in service or removed)

title: Arjo Maxi Sky Series Patient Lift - Lift Not Moving Along Track
description: Lift not traveling along ceiling track due to power, control, obstruction, or track-related issues affecting horizontal movement.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device or surface.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to move the lift along the track using the hand control. Expected: No movement, intermittent movement, or abnormal stopping. Why it matters: Confirms true travel issue vs user misunderstanding.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop (E-Stop)
steps.instructions: Ensure the E-stop button is not engaged. Reset if needed. Expected: Full system power restored and controls responsive. Why it matters: E-stop disables all powered functions, including track movement.

steps.title: Verify Battery Charge and Seating
steps.instructions: Check battery level and ensure it is fully seated. Swap with a known good battery if available. Expected: Adequate charge and stable power. Why it matters: Track drive motor requires sufficient power; low voltage can prevent movement.

steps.title: Test Hand Control Functions
steps.instructions: Confirm other functions (lift up/down) work properly using the same control. Expected: Vertical lift functions operate normally. Why it matters: Helps isolate whether the issue is specific to track drive vs full control failure.

steps.title: Inspect Track for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the entire track path for debris, foreign objects, or physical blockage. Expected: Clear, unobstructed track. Why it matters: Even small obstructions can prevent trolley movement.

steps.title: Check for Track Misalignment or Damage
steps.instructions: Look for bends, gaps, or misaligned sections in the ceiling track. Expected: Smooth, continuous track with no visible damage. Why it matters: Misalignment can bind the trolley and stop movement.

steps.title: Inspect Trolley Wheels and Movement
steps.instructions: Manually attempt slight movement (if safe and unloaded) to feel for binding or resistance. Expected: Smooth, free movement along track. Why it matters: Binding may indicate wheel or internal trolley issue.

steps.title: Check Track Charging/Power Rail (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Ensure charging rails or power contacts are clean and properly aligned. Expected: Clean contacts with no debris or corrosion. Why it matters: Some Maxi Sky units rely on track power for operation.

steps.title: Cycle Power to the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit OFF, wait 10 seconds, then power ON. Expected: System resets and resumes normal operation. Why it matters: Clears minor control or communication faults.

CCR.Complaint: Lift would not travel along ceiling track when using hand control.
CCR.Cause: No external obstruction found; suspected internal track drive motor failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed lift from service and sent to vendor for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Track inspected for obstruction or damage
Final device status (in service vs out of service)

title: Arjo Maxi Sky Series Patient Lift - Charger Rail Not Charging Battery
description: Battery not charging while lift is on rail due to power loss, poor rail contact, or charging system connection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Place the lift on the charging section of the rail and observe charging indicators (LEDs or display). Expected: No charging indication or intermittent charging. Why it matters: Confirms true charging failure vs normal positioning delay.

steps.title: Confirm Rail Power Supply
steps.instructions: Verify the rail system is powered (check wall connection, breaker, or power supply unit if accessible). Expected: Rail system has active power. Why it matters: The charger rail requires facility power to function. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Check Charging Zone Positioning
steps.instructions: Ensure the lift is positioned exactly at the designated charging section of the rail. Slowly move it back and forth to find contact. Expected: Charging indicator activates when correctly positioned. Why it matters: Charging only occurs at specific rail contact points. If charging starts → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Charging Contacts (Lift and Rail)
steps.instructions: Visually inspect contact points on both the lift and rail for dirt, corrosion, or misalignment. Clean gently if needed. Expected: Clean, aligned contacts with solid connection. Why it matters: Poor contact prevents current transfer to the battery. If charging resumes → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions or Damage on Rail
steps.instructions: Look for debris, bent rails, or physical damage at the charging section. Expected: Rail is smooth, clean, and undamaged. Why it matters: Physical issues can prevent proper electrical contact.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Lift or Battery (If Available)
steps.instructions: Place another lift on the same rail or install a known good battery in the unit. Expected: Determine if issue follows the lift or stays with the rail. Why it matters: Helps isolate rail charging system vs lift-side issue.

steps.title: Check Charger Indicator Behavior (If Equipped)
steps.instructions: Observe any charger LEDs or indicators on the lift or rail system. Expected: No lights or abnormal indication may point to charger failure. Why it matters: Indicator status can help differentiate power vs internal fault.

CCR.Complaint: Lift battery not charging when parked on ceiling rail charger.
CCR.Cause: Poor electrical contact at rail charging point due to contaminated contacts.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned charging contacts and verified proper charging function on rail.

helpfulDetails: Alternate lift tested on same rail

title: Arjo Maxi Sky Series Patient Lift - Hand Control or IR Remote Failure
description: Troubleshooting hand control or IR remote failure due to power, connection, or signal issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to operate all lift functions using the hand control or IR remote. Expected: No response, delayed response, or intermittent function. Why it matters: Confirms a true control issue vs user operation misunderstanding.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop (E-Stop)
steps.instructions: Ensure the E-stop is not engaged. Reset if necessary. Expected: Full system power restored and controls responsive. Why it matters: E-stop disables all control inputs, including remote and handset.

steps.title: Verify Lift Power and Battery
steps.instructions: Check battery charge level and ensure it is fully seated. Swap with a known good battery if available. Expected: Stable power with adequate charge. Why it matters: Low or unstable power can prevent control signal processing.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Connection (if wired)
steps.instructions: Check the handset cable connection at the lift. Disconnect and reconnect firmly. Inspect for bent pins, damage, or loose fit. Expected: Secure connection with no visible damage. Why it matters: Loose or damaged connections are a common cause of failure.

steps.title: Check IR Remote Line-of-Sight (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Ensure there are no obstructions between the IR remote and the receiver on the lift. Clean the IR window on both the remote and lift if needed. Expected: Clear line-of-sight and improved responsiveness. Why it matters: IR signals require direct visibility to function properly.

steps.title: Replace or Check Remote Batteries (IR remote only)
steps.instructions: Install fresh batteries in the IR remote and retest. Expected: Immediate response from the lift. Why it matters: Weak batteries reduce signal strength or prevent transmission.

steps.title: Test Alternate Control Method
steps.instructions: If available, test the lift using another hand control or IR remote. Expected: Lift responds normally with alternate control. Why it matters: Helps isolate whether the issue is with the control device or the lift.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Check handset buttons, casing, and cable for wear, cracking, or fluid intrusion. Expected: No visible damage or contamination. Why it matters: Physical damage can prevent signal transmission or button function.

CCR.Complaint: Lift hand control and IR remote not responding to commands.
CCR.Cause: Failed handset cable connection / depleted IR remote batteries (or internal receiver failure if unresolved).
CCR.Resolution: Reseated handset connection and replaced IR remote batteries; restored function (or removed from service for repair if unresolved).

title: Arjo Maxi Sky Series Patient Lift - Motor or drive unit fault
description: Lift not moving, slow movement, or abnormal noise due to power, control, or drive-related external issues affecting motor operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Operate the lift (raise/lower and traverse if applicable) using the hand control. Expected: No movement, slow movement, or abnormal sounds (grinding, clicking). Why it matters: Confirms a true drive issue vs user operation error.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop (E-Stop)
steps.instructions: Ensure the E-stop is not engaged. Reset if necessary. Expected: Full system power restored and controls responsive. Why it matters: E-stop disables all powered movement including the motor. If function returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Battery Charge and Seating
steps.instructions: Check battery level and confirm it is fully seated. Swap with a known good battery if available. Expected: Adequate charge and stable power. Why it matters: Low voltage can mimic motor failure or cause weak/erratic movement. If operation returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Observe for Overload Condition
steps.instructions: Ensure the lift is not exceeding its rated capacity and the sling is properly applied. Expected: Load within safe limits and evenly distributed. Why it matters: Overload protection can prevent motor operation or cause stalling. If corrected and lift operates → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Track and Travel Path (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Check for obstructions, debris, or physical resistance along the track. Manually move unit slightly if safe. Expected: Smooth, unobstructed travel path. Why it matters: Mechanical resistance can strain or stop the drive motor. If obstruction removed and movement returns → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Listen for Motor Activity
steps.instructions: Operate the lift and listen closely for motor noise. Expected: No sound → possible power/control issue; Humming/clicking → motor attempting to run but unable to move. Why it matters: Helps distinguish between electrical control vs mechanical drive issue.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control / Control Input
steps.instructions: Test all functions on the hand control. If available, try an alternate control. Expected: Consistent response across commands. Why it matters: Faulty control input can appear as motor failure. If control issue found and resolved → stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Visible Mechanical Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect drive unit area for signs of damage, unusual noise source, or misalignment (without disassembly). Expected: No visible damage, abnormal vibration, or loose components. Why it matters: External mechanical faults can prevent proper motor operation.

CCR.Complaint: Lift would not move along track and made clicking noise during operation.
CCR.Cause: Motor/drive unit failure after external checks (battery, control, and track) were verified functional.
CCR.Resolution: Removed lift from service and sent to vendor for drive unit repair.

title: Arjo Maxi Sky Series Patient Lift - Track obstruction or trolley binding
description: Troubleshooting lift track movement issues caused by obstruction, debris, or trolley resistance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Use the hand control to move the lift along the track. Observe movement. Expected: Jerky movement, stopping, resistance, or complete lack of travel. Why it matters: Confirms true mechanical restriction vs control or power issue.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop (E-Stop)
steps.instructions: Ensure the E-stop is not engaged. Reset if necessary. Expected: Full system power restored. Why it matters: E-stop disables all powered movement, including track travel.

steps.title: Inspect Track for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the entire accessible track path. Look for debris, foreign objects, or buildup. Expected: Track is clean and unobstructed. Why it matters: Even small debris can prevent smooth trolley movement or cause binding.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Misalignment
steps.instructions: Inspect track sections for bends, dents, gaps at joints, or misalignment. Expected: Track is straight, continuous, and properly aligned. Why it matters: Misalignment can cause the trolley wheels to bind or stop.

steps.title: Inspect Trolley Wheels and Movement
steps.instructions: Observe the trolley as it moves (if accessible). Listen for grinding or resistance. Expected: Smooth, quiet rolling movement. Why it matters: Worn or seized wheels increase resistance and cause binding.

steps.title: Check for Load or Sling Interference
steps.instructions: Ensure sling straps, patient attachments, or accessories are not dragging or catching on the track or motor unit. Expected: Clear path with no interference. Why it matters: External interference can mimic a track or trolley issue.

steps.title: Verify Battery and Power Output
steps.instructions: Check battery charge and seating. Swap with a known good battery if available. Expected: Adequate power for smooth movement. Why it matters: Low power can cause weak motor output, appearing as binding.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Look for ceiling obstructions, rail-mounted accessories, or structural interference along the path. Expected: Clear travel path across entire track. Why it matters: External obstructions are often overlooked but common.

CCR.Complaint: Lift not moving along track and stopping intermittently during transport.
CCR.Cause: Debris found in ceiling track causing trolley binding.
CCR.Resolution: Removed obstruction, cleaned track, and verified smooth operation.

helpfulDetails: Track inspected for debris or obstruction

title: Arjo Sara Stedy Patient Lift - Knee Support Pad Damage or Misalignment
description: Troubleshooting loose, damaged, or misaligned knee support pads causing instability or discomfort during patient transfers.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device. Why it matters: Knee support issues directly affect patient stability and fall risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Inspect and gently apply pressure to the knee support pads. Expected: Pads may feel loose, shifted, cracked, or unstable. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is structural, alignment-related, or cosmetic.

steps.title: Inspect Pad Mounting Hardware
steps.instructions: Check visible screws, brackets, or mounting points securing the knee pads. Expected: Hardware is present, tight, and not stripped or missing. Why it matters: Loose or missing hardware is a common cause of pad misalignment. If loose hardware is found → tighten and retest stability.

steps.title: Check Pad Alignment and Positioning
steps.instructions: Visually confirm both knee pads are level, centered, and aligned with the patient support frame. Expected: Pads are symmetrical and properly oriented. Why it matters: Misalignment can cause uneven weight distribution and patient discomfort. If misaligned → adjust if possible using accessible mounting points.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Examine pads for cracks, tears, compressed foam, or exposed internal material. Expected: Pads should be intact, firm, and properly cushioned. Why it matters: Damaged pads cannot safely support patient knees and may worsen over time. If damage is found → replacement is required.

steps.title: Check for Frame or Support Arm Deformation
steps.instructions: Inspect the metal support structure behind the pads for bending or warping. Expected: Frame is straight with no visible deformation. Why it matters: Structural issues will prevent proper pad alignment and cannot be corrected externally. If deformation is present → escalate to repair.

steps.title: Test Stability Under Light Load (No Patient)
steps.instructions: Apply controlled pressure to simulate use. Expected: Pads remain secure with no shifting or movement. Why it matters: Ensures the fix holds under normal operating conditions. If pads shift or loosen → further repair or replacement required.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported knee support pads were loose and causing patient instability during transfer.
CCR.Cause: Left knee pad mounting hardware loosened, causing misalignment and instability.
CCR.Resolution: Tightened mounting hardware, verified alignment, and confirmed stable operation under load test.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or removed)

title: Arjo Sara Stedy Patient Lift - Base spreader mechanism sticking or not locking
description: Base legs difficult to move, sticking, or not locking in position due to mechanical obstruction, wear, or alignment issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device. Why it matters: Base instability creates a fall risk during transfers.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Manually operate the base spreader mechanism (foot pedal or handle depending on model). Expected: Resistance, sticking, incomplete travel, or failure to lock. Why it matters: Confirms true mechanical issue vs user technique.

steps.title: Inspect for Obstructions or Debris
steps.instructions: Check around the base legs, pivot points, and linkage for debris (hair, dirt, tape, etc.). Expected: Mechanism is clean and unobstructed. Why it matters: Foreign material commonly causes binding or incomplete movement. If debris is found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Check Mechanical Linkage and Pivot Points
steps.instructions: Visually inspect rods, joints, and pivot areas for bending, misalignment, or excessive wear. Expected: Components are straight, aligned, and move freely. Why it matters: Misalignment can cause uneven movement or prevent locking. If minor stiffness is present → apply appropriate facility-approved lubricant and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Locking Mechanism Engagement
steps.instructions: Operate the base fully open and closed and observe if the locking mechanism engages properly. Expected: Audible or tactile “lock” with stable leg position. Why it matters: Failure to lock may indicate worn or damaged locking components. If locking is inconsistent → continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Foot Pedal or Actuation Mechanism (if equipped)
steps.instructions: Inspect the pedal or handle for proper movement, damage, or looseness. Expected: Smooth actuation with full range of motion. Why it matters: A worn or loose actuator may not fully engage the spreader mechanism. If loose hardware is found → tighten and retest.

steps.title: Verify Symmetrical Leg Movement
steps.instructions: Observe both legs during operation to ensure they open and close evenly. Expected: Both legs move simultaneously and evenly. Why it matters: Uneven movement indicates linkage or frame distortion.

CCR.Complaint: Base legs sticking and not locking during patient transfer.
CCR.Cause: Debris buildup and stiffness at pivot points causing incomplete linkage movement.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned mechanism, applied approved lubricant, verified smooth operation and proper locking.

helpfulDetails: Presence of debris in pivot areas
Condition of linkage rods and joints
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Arjo Sara Stedy Patient Lift - Brake Failure or Caster Locking Issues
description: Troubleshooting brakes not holding or caster locking issues due to debris, wear, or mechanical problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device. Why it matters: Uncontrolled movement creates a high fall risk during transfers.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Engage and disengage each caster brake individually. Attempt to move the lift. Expected: Brakes should firmly hold when engaged and release fully when disengaged. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is isolated to one caster or system-wide.

steps.title: Inspect for Debris Around Casters
steps.instructions: Check wheels and brake mechanisms for hair, dirt, tape, or buildup. Expected: Casters rotate freely and brake components are clear. Why it matters: Debris is a common cause of incomplete brake engagement or sticking. If debris is found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Check Brake Pedal/Lever Function
steps.instructions: Operate the brake pedal or lever and observe movement of the locking mechanism. Expected: Smooth engagement/disengagement with full travel. Why it matters: Limited travel may prevent proper locking of the wheel.

steps.title: Inspect Caster Condition
steps.instructions: Check for worn wheels, flat spots, cracks, or damaged locking tabs. Expected: Wheels are intact and brake surfaces are not excessively worn. Why it matters: Worn components reduce friction and prevent effective braking. If damage is found → caster replacement required.

steps.title: Verify Floor Surface Conditions
steps.instructions: Test the lift on a clean, dry, level surface. Expected: Brakes hold effectively on proper flooring. Why it matters: Wet or uneven floors can mimic brake failure.

steps.title: Check Mounting and Stability of Casters
steps.instructions: Inspect caster mounting points for looseness or misalignment. Expected: Casters are secure and aligned properly with the base. Why it matters: Loose casters can prevent full brake engagement and cause instability. If loose → tighten hardware and retest.

steps.title: Test All Casters Together
steps.instructions: Engage all brakes and attempt to move the lift. Expected: Lift remains stationary with no rolling or shifting. Why it matters: Ensures system-wide stability during patient transfers.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported lift rolling even with brakes engaged.
CCR.Cause: Right rear caster brake worn and not fully engaging wheel.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced faulty caster and verified proper brake function.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (operational or removed from service)

title: Arjo Sara Stedy Patient Lift - Frame Joint Looseness or Instability
description: Troubleshooting frame looseness or instability caused by hardware issues, wear, or structural fatigue.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device. Why it matters: Frame instability creates a high fall and injury risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Gently push and pull on the frame (handle, base, knee support area) to identify movement. Expected: Noticeable looseness, wobble, or shifting at joints. Why it matters: Confirms true instability and helps localize the affected area.

steps.title: Inspect All Visible Fasteners and Hardware
steps.instructions: Check bolts, screws, and mounting points at key joints (base, vertical column, handle assembly). Expected: Hardware should be present, tight, and secure. Why it matters: Loose or missing hardware is the most common cause of frame instability. If loose hardware is found → tighten to secure fit and retest stability. If missing hardware is found → remove from service until replaced.

steps.title: Check for Worn or Enlarged Mounting Points
steps.instructions: Inspect holes, brackets, and connection points for elongation, cracking, or wear. Expected: Mounting points should be round, intact, and not deformed. Why it matters: Worn mounting points prevent proper tightening and indicate structural fatigue. If deformation is found → remove from service.

steps.title: Inspect Welded Joints and Frame Integrity
steps.instructions: Visually examine welds and frame tubing for cracks, separation, or bending. Expected: Welds should be intact with no visible cracks or gaps. Why it matters: Weld failure compromises the entire structural integrity of the lift. If any crack or separation is found → immediately remove from service.

steps.title: Check Base and Vertical Column Connection
steps.instructions: Focus on the connection between the base and upright column for movement. Expected: No independent movement between base and column. Why it matters: This is a primary load-bearing joint; looseness here is critical.

steps.title: Assess for Excessive Wear or Long-Term Fatigue
steps.instructions: Consider age, usage level, and history of the device. Expected: Frame should remain rigid under normal handling. Why it matters: High-use devices can develop fatigue even without visible damage. If repeated loosening occurs → likely structural degradation.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported Sara Stedy lift felt loose and unstable during patient transfer.
CCR.Cause: Loose frame hardware found at base-to-column joint; no structural cracks observed.
CCR.Resolution: Tightened hardware, verified stability, and returned unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Presence of cracks or frame damage
Final device status (in service vs out of service)

title: Arjo Sara Stedy Patient Lift - Footplate Wear or Detachment
description: Footplate loose, worn, cracked, or detaching causing patient instability or unsafe footing during transfers.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the lift from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely transferred to another device. Why it matters: Footplate failure creates a direct fall and injury risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Visually inspect and apply light pressure to the footplate. Expected: Signs of looseness, cracking, shifting, or detachment. Why it matters: Confirms structural vs cosmetic issue.

steps.title: Inspect Footplate Mounting Points
steps.instructions: Check where the footplate connects to the base frame (bolts, brackets, or hinge points). Expected: Hardware is present, tight, and secure. Why it matters: Loose or missing hardware is a common cause of instability. If loose hardware is found → tighten and retest. If hardware is missing → remove from service until replaced.

steps.title: Check for Cracks or Structural Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the entire footplate surface and underside for cracks, splits, or deformation. Expected: Solid structure with no visible damage. Why it matters: Structural damage cannot safely support patient weight. If cracks or damage are found → remove from service immediately.

steps.title: Assess Footplate Stability Under Load (No Patient)
steps.instructions: Apply downward pressure simulating foot placement. Expected: No flexing, shifting, or movement. Why it matters: Confirms integrity under expected use conditions. If instability is present → remove from service.

steps.title: Inspect Anti-Slip Surface Condition
steps.instructions: Check for worn, peeling, or smooth surfaces where traction is reduced. Expected: Surface provides adequate grip. Why it matters: Poor traction increases slip risk during transfers. If worn → replace footplate or apply approved replacement surface if applicable.

steps.title: Check Alignment with Base Frame
steps.instructions: Ensure the footplate sits level and properly aligned with the lift base. Expected: Even positioning with no tilt or misalignment. Why it matters: Misalignment can indicate mounting failure or frame distortion.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported footplate felt loose and unstable during patient transfer.
CCR.Cause: Footplate mounting bolts found loose and anti-slip surface worn.
CCR.Resolution: Tightened hardware, verified stability, and scheduled footplate replacement due to wear.

helpfulDetails: Presence of cracks or structural damage
Final device status (in service or removed)
